perturbation.original_text: This amendment to Section 2 (titled "Term"), Part B of the Co-Branding Agreement is made effective December 9, 1996 by and between PC Quote, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "PCQ") and A.B. Watley, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "ABW"), who are also the parties contracted in the aforementioned Co-Branding Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This amendment to Section 2 (titled "Term"), Part B of the Co-Branding Agreement is made effective December 9, 1996 by and between PC Quote, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "PCQ") and A.B. Watley, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "Partner"), who are also the parties contracted in the aforementioned Co-Branding Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'ABW' is changed to 'Partner'. This creates confusion because the agreement initially defines 'ABW' as A.B. Watley, Inc., but later refers to the same entity as 'Partner' without explicitly stating they are the same. This introduces ambiguity regarding which entity is bound by the agreement, particularly if the term 'Partner' is used elsewhere in a broader context. It creates uncertainty in the context of which entity is being referenced for obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This amendment to Section 2 (titled "Term"), Part B of the Co-Branding Agreement is made effective December 9, 1996 by and between PC Quote, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "PCQ") and A.B. Watley, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "Partner"), who are also the parties contracted in the aforementioned Co-Branding Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'ABW' with 'Partner' as the defined term for A.B. Watley, Inc., while the contradicted text references both 'PCQ' and 'Partner' as the parties contracted. The agreement uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them (A.B. Watley, Inc. is no longer clearly tied to Partner), leading to conflicting obligations, which meets the definition of Misaligned Terminology leading to In-Text Contradiction. This creates ambiguity about which entity is bound by the agreement, potentially leading to disputes and uncertainty about contractual obligations.

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall apply to said PCQ and ABW and all of their subsidiaries and related companies.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment shall apply to said PCQ and their Co-Branding counterpart and all of their subsidiaries and related companies.
perturbation.explanation: Replaces 'ABW' with 'their Co-Branding counterpart'. This creates an in-text contradiction. While 'ABW' is initially defined as A.B. Watley, Inc., this change introduces the phrase 'their Co-Branding counterpart', which is not explicitly defined, creating ambiguity as to who 'PCQ's Co-Branding counterpart' is. It creates confusion as to which entity is subject to the terms of the amendment, potentially affecting enforcement and liability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: This Amendment shall apply to said PCQ and their Co-Branding counterpart and all of their subsidiaries and related companies.
perturbation.justification: The Changed Text and Contradicted Text are identical, indicating that the changed text was simply repeated elsewhere. The explanation states that replacing 'ABW' with 'their Co-Branding counterpart' introduces ambiguity because the latter term is not defined. The contradiction arises from the term 'their Co-Branding counterpart' being undefined while potentially carrying legal weight, introducing ambiguity. However, while there is ambiguity, the introduction of 'their Co-Branding counterpart' alongside the already present 'PCQ' doesn't create a direct contradiction with the contradicted text itself. It simply introduces a new undefined element. If there was a separate definition of ABW and then this text replaced it, then that would be a contradiction. Based on the guidelines, this does not cause a strong contradiction within the document.

perturbation.original_text: AGREED TO BY: /s/ Steven Malin /s/ Howard Meltzer - -------------------------- --------------------------------- A.B. Watley, Inc. PC Quote, Inc. Mr. Steven Malin Mr. Howard Meltzer Director President Date: 12/5/96 Date: 12/12/96 -------------- ---------------- Source: PCQUOTE COM INC, S-1/A, 7/21/1999
perturbation.changed_text: AGREED TO BY: /s/ Steven Malin /s/ Howard Meltzer - -------------------------- --------------------------------- Co-Branding Entity PC Quote, Inc. Mr. Steven Malin Mr. Howard Meltzer Director President Date: 12/5/96 Date: 12/12/96 -------------- ---------------- Source: PCQUOTE COM INC, S-1/A, 7/21/1999
perturbation.explanation: Replaces 'A.B. Watley, Inc.' with 'Co-Branding Entity'. This creates a contradiction. 'A.B. Watley, Inc.' is a clearly defined entity, whilst 'Co-Branding Entity' is not defined. This replacement creates ambiguity about which specific entity has agreed to the amendment, weakening the legal certainty of the agreement. This creates uncertainty in the parties agreeing.
perturbation.contradicted_text: AGREED TO BY: /s/ Steven Malin /s/ Howard Meltzer - -------------------------- --------------------------------- Co-Branding Entity PC Quote, Inc. Mr. Steven Malin Mr. Howard Meltzer Director President Date: 12/5/96 Date: 12/12/96 -------------- ---------------- Source: PCQUOTE COM INC, S-1/A, 7/21/1999
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'A.B. Watley, Inc.' with 'Co-Branding Entity'. While the explanation states this creates ambiguity, the contradicted text is identical to the changed text. The contradiction exists in both places, but the changed text doesn't introduce it. The contradiction is already present in the contradicted text and was introduced with the Changed Text. The Misaligned Terminology definition states that this happens when the contract uses multiple terms interchangeably without defining them. However, since the changed text is the same as the contradicted text, there is no contradiction created between the changed text and the contradicted text. They're both the same. Therefore, the answer is NO.
